The solution of (15) subject to x 1 (0) = 0, ẋ 1 (0) = 0 is x 1 ( t ) = − t sin t , (16) which is a secular term, i.e., a term that grows without bound as t → ∞. In summary, the solution of (5) , (6) according to perturbation theory is x ( t , ε ) = sin t − ε t sin t + O ( ε 2 ) . (17) How does this compare with the exact solution (7) ? In Exercise 7.6.1, you are asked to show that the two formulas agree in the following sense: If (7) is expanded as power series in ε , the first two terms are given by (17) . In fact, (17) is the beginning of a convergent series expansion for the true solution. For any fixed t , (17) provides a good approximation as long as ε is small enough—specifically, we need εt ≪ 1 so that the correction term (which is actually O ( ε 2 t 2 )) is negligible. But normally we are interested in the behavior for fixed ε , not fixed t. In that case we can only expect the perturbation approximation to work for times t ≪ O (1/ ε ). To illustrate this limitation, Figure 7.6.2 plots the exact solution (7) and the perturbation series (17) for ε = 0.1. As expected, the perturbation series works reasonably well if t < < 1 ε = 10 , but it breaks down after that. Figure 7.6.2 In many situations we’d like our approximation to capture the true solution’s qualitative behavior for all t , or at least for large t . By this criterion, (17) is a failure, as Figure 7.6.2 makes obvious. There are two major problems: 1.  The true solution (7) exhibits two time scales: a fast time t ~ O (1) for the sinusoidal oscillations and a slow time t ~ 1/ ε over which the amplitude decays. Equation (17) completely misrepresents the slow time scale behavior. In particular, because of the secular term t sin t , (17) falsely suggests that the solution grows with time whereas we know from (7) that the amplitude A = (l − ε 2 ) −1/2 e − εt decays exponentially. The discrepancy occurs because e − εt = 1 − εt + O ( ε 2 t 2 ), so to this order in ε , it appears (incorrectly) that the amplitude increases with t. To get the correct result, we’d need to calculate an infinite number of terms in the series. That’s worthless; we want series approximations that work well with just one or two terms. 2.  The frequency of the oscillations in (7) is ω = ( 1 − ε 2 ) 1 / 2 ≈ 1 − 1 2 ε 2 , which is shifted slightly from the frequency ω = 1 of (17) . After a very long time t ~ O (1 / ε 2 ), this frequency error will have a significant cumulative effect. Note that this is a third, super-slow time scale! Two-Timing The elementary example above reveals a more general truth: There are going to be (at least) two time scales in weakly nonlinear oscillators. We’ve already met this phenomenon in Figure 7.6.1 , where the amplitude of the spiral grew very slowly compared to the cycle time. An analytical method called two-timing builds in the fact of two time scales from the start, and produces better approximations than regular perturbation theory. In fact, more than two times can be used, but we’ll stick to the simplest case. To apply two-timing to (1) , let τ = t denote the fast O (1) time, and let T = εt denote the slow time. We’ll treat these two times as if they were independent variables. In particular, functions of the slow time T will be regarded as constants on the fast time scale τ . It’s hard to justify this idea rigorously, but it works! (Here’s an analogy: it’s like saying that your height is constant on the time scale of a day. Of course, over many months or years your height can change dramatically, especially if you’re an infant or a pubescent teenager, but over one day your height stays constant, to a good approximation.) Now we turn to the mechanics of the method. We expand the solution of (1) as a series x ( t , ε ) = x 0 ( τ , T ) + ε x 1 ( τ , T ) + O ( ε 2 ) . (18) The time derivatives in (1) are transformed using the chain rule: x ˙ = d x d t = ∂ x ∂ τ + ∂ x ∂ T ∂ T ∂ t = ∂ x ∂ τ + ε ∂ x ∂ T . (19) A subscript notation for differentiation is more compact; thus we write (19) as x ˙ = ∂ τ x + ε ∂ T x . (20) After substituting (18) into (20) and collecting powers of ε , we find x ˙ = ∂ τ x 0 + ε ( ∂ T x 0 + ∂ τ x 1 ) + O ( ε 2 ) . (21) Similarly, x ¨ = ∂ τ τ x 0 + ε ( ∂ τ τ x 1 + 2 ∂ T τ x 0 ) + O ( ε 2 ) . (22) To illustrate the method, let’s apply it to our earlier test problem. EXAMPLE 7.6.1: Use two-timing to approximate the solution to the damped linear oscillator ẍ + 2εẋ + x = 0, with initial conditions x (0) = 0, ẋ (0) = 1. Solution: After substituting (21) and (22) for ẋ and ẍ, we get ∂ τ τ x 0 + ε ( ∂ τ τ x 1 + 2 ∂ T τ
